scenarios.name: Specific Performance of Sale Agreement

checks.detail: Specific Relief Act / specific performance cited
At least one statutory section cited
Prayer for execution of sale deed
No invented amounts (10L/50L never in facts)

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE CITY CIVIL COURT AT BENGALURU

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

Anita Sharma,
Aged about 52 years,
Residing at: Indiranagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

Rajesh Reddy,
Aged about 60 years,
Residing at: HSR Layout, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF CONTRACT WITH COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court at Bengaluru, and the Defendant resides within such territorial limits.

3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court.

COMMERCIAL COURTS ACT COMPLIANCE

4. The Plaintiff states that the present suit is a commercial dispute within the meaning of Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. The Plaintiff has complied with the mandatory pre-institution mediation process under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and the same stands concluded / exempted. 

LIMITATION

5. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 54 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years from date fixed for performance, or if no date fixed, when plaintiff has notice that performance is refused. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

FACTS OF THE CASE

6. On 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into a written Agreement to Sell regarding the residential property bearing Flat No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft, bearing Survey Number {{PROPERTY_SURVEY_NUMBER}} (hereinafter referred to as the "Schedule Property"). The total sale consideration was fixed at Rs. 85,00,000/-. A copy of the Agreement to Sell is marked as Annexure A.

7. Pursuant to the terms of the Agreement to Sell, the Plaintiff paid an advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- to the Defendant on 10.01.2024 via RTGS bearing UTR No. HDFC20240110987654. The receipt of the said payment is evidenced by the bank document marked as Annexure B.

8. As per the covenants in the Agreement to Sell, the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- was agreed to be paid by the Plaintiff on or before 10.07.2024, against the execution and registration of the Sale Deed. The Plaintiff has always been ready and willing to perform her part of the contract and tender the balance consideration within the stipulated timeline.

9. The Defendant acknowledged the receipt of the advance and agreed to convey the title upon receipt of the balance payment. The Plaintiff maintained all necessary funds ready for the completion of the transaction as per the agreed schedule.

BREACH OF CONTRACT

10. On 05.06.2024, prior to the deadline for payment of the balance consideration, the Defendant issued a letter explicitly refusing to execute the Sale Deed. The Defendant cited the receipt of a higher offer from a third party as the reason for such refusal, thereby committing a clear breach of the contractual obligations. A copy of the Defendant's refusal letter is marked as Annexure C.

11. Aggrieved by the Defendant's refusal, the Plaintiff issued a Legal Notice dated 20.06.2024 calling upon the Defendant to specifically perform the contract and execute the Sale Deed as agreed. The Defendant received the notice but failed to comply with the requisition or execute the Sale Deed, thereby confirming his unwillingness to perform the contract. A copy of the Legal Notice and postal receipt is marked as Annexure D.

DAMAGES

12. Due to the wrongful refusal and breach of contract by the Defendant, the Plaintiff has suffered significant financial hardship. The advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- remains locked with the Defendant without any return or interest, depriving the Plaintiff of the opportunity to invest the same elsewhere or purchase an alternative property during this period.

13. The Plaintiff has been compelled to institute the present suit to enforce her legal rights, incurring substantial legal costs and expenses. The Plaintiff is entitled to recover the costs of the suit and compensation for the delay and inconvenience caused by the Defendant's unjustified refusal to perform the contract.

LEGAL BASIS

14. The Plaintiff is entitled to a decree of specific performance of the agreement under Sections 10 and 14 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963, the subject matter being such that the grant of compensation in money would not afford adequate relief.

15. The Plaintiff has been ready and willing to perform her part of the agreement at all material times within the meaning of Section 16(c) of the Specific Relief Act, 1963.

16. The present suit is maintainable under Section 9 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

CAUSE OF ACTION

17. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 05.06.2024 when the Defendant committed the breach / wrongful act giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to institute the present suit.

18. The cause of action further arose when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice dated 20.06.2024, failed and refused to comply with the Plaintiff's demand.

19. The cause of action is continuing in nature until the Defendant performs his contractual obligations.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

20. The suit is valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. Appropriate court fee has been paid on this plaint under the provisions of the Court Fees Act, 1870 as applicable in the State of Karnataka. The plaint is properly stamped.

INTEREST

21. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:

    (a) Pendente lite interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of filing of this suit till the date of judgment / decree, under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (b) Future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of judgment / decree till the date of realisation of the entire decretal amount.

PRAYER

In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree of specific performance directing the Defendant to perform and complete the agreement dated 10.01.2024 in favour of the Plaintiff;
    (b) Direct the Defendant to execute and register the necessary conveyance / sale deed in favour of the Plaintiff upon receipt of the balance consideration;
    (c) In the alternative, award compensation / damages in lieu of specific performance;
    (d) Award costs of the suit;
    (e) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Original agreement dated 10.01.2024

Annexure B — RTGS receipt (UTR: HDFC20240110987654)

Annexure C — Defendant's refusal letter dated 05.06.2024

Annexure D — Legal notice dated 20.06.2024 with postal receipt

VERIFICATION

Verified at {{CITY}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 21 of this plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and no material facts have been concealed therefrom, and that I have not filed any other suit, petition, or proceeding in any court or before any authority in respect of the same subject matter and cause of action.

DEPONENT

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

PLAINTIFF

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE Commercial Court (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT Bengaluru

Commercial Suit No. __________ / 20____

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd,
A company registered under the Companies Act,
Having its registered office at: MG Road, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Director Priya Menon, aged 40 years.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

CloudBridge Systems LLP,
A limited liability partnership having its office at Whitefield, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Designated Partner Vikram Singh, aged 48 years.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

COMMERCIAL COURT MAINTAINABILITY

1. The subject matter of the present suit arises out of a commercial dispute within the meaning of Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. The suit is valued above the specified value of Rs. 3,00,000/- as prescribed under Section 12 of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and is therefore maintainable before this Hon'ble Commercial Court.

JURISDICTION

2. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

3. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court at Bengaluru, and the Defendant carries on business / resides within such territorial limits.

4. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty Two Lakhs Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court. This Hon'ble Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain commercial disputes under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

LIMITATION

5. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years from when contract is broken; or for continuing breach, when it ceases. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

6. The Plaintiff states that the mandatory pre-institution mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 has been complied with.

FACTS OF THE CASE

7. On 01.02.2024, the Plaintiff entered into a Software Development Agreement with the Defendant for the development of a customized ERP system. The total contract value agreed upon between the parties was Rs. 45,00,000/-. The terms and conditions governing the relationship between the Plaintiff and the Defendant are recorded in the said Agreement, marked as Annexure A.

8. Pursuant to the terms of the Agreement marked as Annexure A, the Plaintiff paid a sum of Rs. 20,00,000/- to the Defendant as the first milestone payment on 01.02.2024. The said payment was made via NEFT bearing UTR Number ICIC20240201456789. The proof of the said transaction is marked as Annexure B.

9. As per the agreed timeline stipulated in the Agreement marked as Annexure A, the Defendant was contractually obligated to deliver the first module of the ERP system on or before 01.05.2024. The timely delivery of the said module was essential for the Plaintiff's business operations.

10. The software was intended to streamline the Plaintiff's operational processes. The Plaintiff relied upon the Defendant's expertise and assurances regarding the delivery schedule to plan its business activities accordingly.

PARTICULARS OF BREACH

11. The Defendant failed to deliver the first module of the ERP system by the agreed deadline of 01.05.2024. Despite the lapse of the stipulated time, no deliverables were provided to the Plaintiff by the Defendant.

12. On 15.06.2024, the Defendant communicated its decision to abandon the project without completing the agreed scope of work. This action constitutes a breach of the terms of the Agreement marked as Annexure A. The email correspondence evidencing the abandonment of the project is marked as Annexure C.

13. Following the abandonment of the project, the Defendant failed to refund the advance payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- received from the Plaintiff. The Defendant has retained the said amount without justification despite the non-performance of its contractual obligations.

14. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice to the Defendant dated 01.07.2024, calling upon the Defendant to refund the advance amount and compensate for the losses suffered. A copy of the said legal notice is marked as Annexure E.

15. The Defendant has failed to respond satisfactorily to the legal notice marked as Annexure E. The failure to respond and rectify the breach has compelled the Plaintiff to institute the present suit.

DAMAGES

16. The Plaintiff is entitled to the refund of the advance payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- paid to the Defendant. The Defendant's failure to perform the contract necessitates the return of the said principal amount.

17. Due to the non-delivery of the ERP system and the subsequent delay in operations, the Plaintiff has suffered consequential losses quantified at Rs. 12,00,000/-. The assessment of the said loss is based on the loss assessment report prepared by the auditor, marked as Annexure D.

18. The Plaintiff is entitled to interest on the claimed amounts at the rate of 18% per annum from the date of breach until the date of realisation. The detention of the Plaintiff's funds by the Defendant has caused financial prejudice warranting the award of interest.

LEGAL BASIS

19. The Defendant has committed a breach of the agreement by failing to perform the obligations undertaken therein, thereby entitling the Plaintiff to claim damages under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.

20. The Defendant has committed a repudiatory breach by refusing to perform its obligations, entitling the Plaintiff to treat the contract as at an end and seek appropriate relief.

21. The Plaintiff is entitled to compensation under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which provides that when a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive compensation for any loss or damage caused thereby, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach, or which the parties knew, when they made the contract, to be likely to result from the breach of it.

22. The present suit is maintainable under Section 9 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

CAUSE OF ACTION

23. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant committed the breach / wrongful act giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to institute the present suit.

24. The cause of action further arose when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice dated 01.07.2024, failed and refused to comply with the Plaintiff's demand.

25. The cause of action is not a continuing cause of action. The breach consisted of a single event. However, the Plaintiff's right to damages subsists.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

26. The suit is valued at Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty Two Lakhs Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. Appropriate court fee has been paid on this plaint under the provisions of the Court Fees Act, 1870 as applicable in the State of Karnataka. The plaint is properly stamped.

INTEREST

27. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:

    (a) Pendente lite interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of filing of this suit till the date of judgment / decree, under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (b) Future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of judgment / decree till the date of realisation of the entire decretal amount.

PRAYER

In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree in
